file.content:
# ROADMAP: gem5 CPU-Switch Parity

Tracks 11 independent features to extend hybrid_sim (QEMU + vsim) with the four
CPU-switch mechanisms from `specs/hybrid-sim-gem5-switch-parity.md`. One
session = one feature. Update status when starting (in-progress) and on
completion (done).

## Status Legend

- not-started: feature has not begun
- in-progress: currently being implemented in a session
- done: behavioural+structural commits landed and tests pass

## Dependency DAG

```
F1 ----+----> F4
F2 ----+
F3 ----+
F5 ----+----> F6
       +----> F7
       +----> F8 ----> F9 ----> F10
       +----> F11
```

Migration order: ship F1, F2, F3 first (infrastructure layer). After that,
F5/F6/F7 parallelise across sessions because they touch independent files.

## Features

### F1 - Rebrand plugin drain-exit code 0 -> 200

- Status: done
- Type: structural
- Description: Today `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c:261` exits 0 on drain;
  workload completion and drain are indistinguishable. vsim already exits
  200 (`hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/exit_codes.hpp:7`). Bring the plugin in
  line. The orchestrator (F2+) relies on this distinction.
- Key files:
  - `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c:261`
  - `hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/rt_shared_mem.sh` (exit-code assertions)
  - `hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/roundtrip_e2e.sh`
- Dependencies: none
- TDD RED: a test in `tests/hybrid/test_plugin_drain_exit_code.sh` runs
  the plugin with a CSR fixture and asserts QEMU exits with code 200.
  Fails on today's 0.

### F2 - Orchestrator skeleton (Python package)

- Status: done
- Type: structural (new code)
- Description: Create `tools/orchestrator/` Python package. CLI parsing,
  plan loading from YAML, dry-run that prints the planned spawns. No real
  spawn yet.
- Key files:
  - `tools/orchestrator/__init__.py` (new)
  - `tools/orchestrator/main.py` (new)
  - `tools/orchestrator/plan.py` (new)
  - `tools/orchestrator/examples/csr_oneshot.yaml` (new)
- Dependencies: none
- TDD RED: `tools/orchestrator/tests/test_plan_parses.py` loads
  `examples/csr_oneshot.yaml` and asserts the resulting `Session` has
  three `Step` entries. Fails because no parser exists.

### F3 - ctypes ABI binding + state-file validator

- Status: done
- Type: structural (new code)
- Description: Mirror `include/hybrid/state_abi.h`'s `hybrid_state_v1` in
  Python via ctypes. Field-offset asserts at import time. Add
  `validate_state_file(path)` checking magic 0x42594841, version 1,
  exact `sizeof(hybrid_state_v1)` bytes. Wire into orchestrator as the
  post-spawn gate.
- Key files:
  - `tools/orchestrator/abi.py` (new)
  - `include/hybrid/state_abi.h` (read-only)
  - `include/hybrid/state_abi_check.h` (read-only; mirror its asserts)
- Dependencies: F2
- TDD RED: `tools/orchestrator/tests/test_abi_roundtrip.py` writes a known
  `hybrid_state_v1` struct from a small C harness, reads it in Python,
  asserts all fields (GPRs, FPRs, CSRs, V-state) match. Fails until
  ctypes layout matches C.

### F4 - Orchestrator runs today's CSR fixture end-to-end

- Status: done (Python gdbstub replaces the planned `hybrid_apply_state`
  C++ CLI; the in-process spawn at `main.cpp:478-490` is left in place so
  the existing bash harness keeps working - deletion is a follow-up.)
- Type: behavioral
- Description: Replace one `rt_shared_mem.sh` invocation with
  `orchestrator run <yaml>` for `rt_c_v_regs`. Add the C++ helper
  `tools/hybrid_apply_state` extracted from `qemu_handback.hpp`'s
  state-restore body. Delete the in-process spawn at `main.cpp:478-490`;
  vsim returns 200 cleanly.
- Key files:
  - `tools/orchestrator/spawn.py` (new)
  - `tools/orchestrator/main.py`
  - `tools/hybrid_apply_state/` (new C++ CLI; lifts from
    `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/qemu_handback.hpp:287-335 apply_state`,
    `:41-63 pick_free_port`)
  - `hybrid_vsim/src/main.cpp:478-490` (delete)
  - `hybrid_vsim/cmake/HybridConfig.cmake:108-238` (add CTest variant)
- Dependencies: F1, F2, F3
- TDD RED: `ctest -R rt_c_v_regs--orchestrator` is registered but the
  orchestrator's spawn path is incomplete; test fails. Then asserts
  exit-code parity with `rt_c_v_regs--csr` (the bash-harness version).

### F5 - icount counter + trigger in QEMU plugin

- Status: done. Trigger priority (spec section 5 row 1: CSR/PC > BBV >
  icount) is deferred - F5 registers icount tick FIRST in
  `vcpu_tb_trans`, so it wins on insns where both fire. The F5 e2e uses
  a CSR-less fixture, so this is invisible today; F11 (slice-consume)
  will revisit when BBV ties enter the picture.
- Type: behavioral
- Description: Add `uint64_t icount_retired` incremented in a per-insn
  callback registered alongside the CSR/PC matcher in
  `vcpu_tb_trans()`. New plugin arg `icount=N`. When
  `icount_retired >= icount_target`, set the should-exit flag and call
  `on_handoff` at the same insn boundary.
- Key files:
  - `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c:264-300` (`vcpu_tb_trans`)
  - `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c:302-339` (arg parser)
- Dependencies: F1
- TDD RED: `tests/hybrid/test_plugin_icount_trigger.sh` runs a fixture
  with `--plugin-args icount=100000` on a CSR-less binary; asserts the
  drained state file's `minstret >= 100000` and exit code is 200. Fails
  because the `icount=` arg is unknown.

### F6 - vsim IcountTrigger (sibling of CsrTrigger)

- Status: done. `HandoffController` was retrofitted to a class template
  on the trigger type (structural; CTAD preserves all existing call
  sites). `IcountTrigger` lives at `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/icount_trigger.hpp`
  with the duck-type surface (`lookup`, `empty`, `observe_commit`).
  `--hybrid-icount N` is wired in `main.cpp` and dispatches into
  `Simulator::enable_hybrid_icount_handoff`. Mutually exclusive with
  `--hybrid-exit-pc`. Unit test
  `tests/hybrid/icount_trigger.test.cpp` covers both the standalone
  trigger and the templated controller's drain.
- Type: behavioral
- Description: New `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/icount_trigger.hpp` with the
  same duck-type surface as `CsrTrigger` (`lookup(pc) ->
  std::optional<hit>`, plus an `observe_commit()` that increments the
  per-hart counter). `HandoffController` is already template-parameterised
  on trigger type (`handoff_controller.hpp:38`). Add
  `--hybrid-icount N` to `main.cpp:466-472`. Multi-hart out of scope
  (plan-validation rejects `hart_id != 0`).
- Key files:
  - `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/icount_trigger.hpp` (new)
  - `hybrid_vsim/src/main.cpp:466-472`
  - `hybrid_vsim/src/simulator.hpp:223-253` (registration)
- Dependencies: none (parallel to F5)
- TDD RED: `hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/test_icount_trigger.cpp` (doctest)
  builds a `RecordingBus` + `IcountTrigger(N=100)`, drives 100 retire
  ticks, asserts `on_retire` returns a drained state at exactly the
  100th. Fails because the class does not exist.

### F7 - BBV emission plugin (profile mode)

- Status: not-started
- Type: behavioral
- Description: BBV emission as a second sub-mode of `hybrid_handoff`
  (not a separate plugin - keeps per-insn callback cost to one
  per insn). New plugin args `bbv_out=PATH,slice=N`. Emit one BBV row
  per `slice` dynamic insns into a SimPoint-readable `.bb` file.
- Key files:
  - `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c`
  - `qemu_plugin/bbv_emit.c` (new sub-module)
  - `qemu_plugin/Makefile`
- Dependencies: F5 (reuses the icount counter)
- TDD RED: `tests/hybrid/test_bbv_emit.sh` runs a fixture with
  `bbv_out=/tmp/x.bb,slice=10000`; asserts the file is SimPoint-format
  and has the correct number of slice rows for the binary's dynamic
  insn count.

### F8 - Orchestrator N-way switching with YAML plan grammar

- Status: not-started
- Type: behavioral
- Description: Parse the full `steps:` grammar. Implement the spawn
  loop: spawn, wait, validate state file, archive (no-op if no
  `archive_as`), advance. Pass `--state-in` from previous spawn's
  `--state-out`. Mmap fd held open across all spawns.
- Key files:
  - `tools/orchestrator/plan.py`
  - `tools/orchestrator/main.py`
  - `tools/orchestrator/spawn.py`
  - `tools/orchestrator/mmap_owner.py` (new)
- Dependencies: F4
- TDD RED: `tools/orchestrator/tests/test_two_step_session.py` runs a
  2-step plan (QEMU -> vsim -> QEMU) on `rt_c_v_regs`; asserts both
  intermediate state files validate. Fails until the loop exists.

### F9 - Checkpoint archive write

- Status: not-started
- Type: behavioral
- Description: When a step has `archive_as: snap/N`, after the spawn
  exits, copy `state.bin` and the mmap into `snap/N/`; write
  `meta.json`. Reflink-or-copy by filesystem probe (btrfs/xfs use
  `cp --reflink=always`; ext4 falls back to sequential `cp`).
- Key files:
  - `tools/orchestrator/checkpoint.py` (new)
  - `tools/orchestrator/main.py`
- Dependencies: F8
- TDD RED:
  `tools/orchestrator/tests/test_checkpoint_archive.py` runs a 1-step
  plan with `archive_as: snap/0`; asserts the three artefacts exist
  and `state.bin` byte-matches the in-flight one.

### F10 - Checkpoint replay (`replay-checkpoint <dir>`)

- Status: not-started
- Type: behavioral
- Description: New orchestrator entry point. Validates `meta.json`
  against the live binary's CPU variant and VLEN. Hydrates a new
  `/dev/shm` mmap from `snap/N/mem`. Copies `state.bin` to working
  path. Spawns the requested model. No plugin or vsim code change.
- Key files:
  - `tools/orchestrator/checkpoint.py`
  - `tools/orchestrator/main.py`
- Dependencies: F9
- TDD RED:
  `tools/orchestrator/tests/test_checkpoint_replay.py` writes a known
  archive (using F9), runs `replay-checkpoint snap/0 --resume-with
  vsim`; asserts the spawned vsim drains a state file whose GPRs
  match the archive.

### F11 - BBV slice-consume trigger

- Status: not-started
- Type: behavioral
- Description: Third sub-mode of `hybrid_handoff` (after CSR/PC,
  icount, BBV-emit): read a slice-index list, fire drain when the
  icount counter hits each slice index. Orchestrator chains N
  invocations through the plan grammar to produce K archived regions
  per SimPoint cluster.
- Key files:
  - `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c`
  - `qemu_plugin/bbv_emit.c`
  - `tools/orchestrator/plan.py` (new trigger kind `phase`)
- Dependencies: F5, F7, F8
- TDD RED: `tests/hybrid/test_plugin_phase_consume.sh` supplies a
  3-slice list; asserts the plugin drains at exactly the third slice
  (verified by the state file's `minstret`).

## Out of Scope (deferred to future waves)

- Multi-hart trigger support. Plan-validation in F8 explicitly rejects
  `hart_id != 0`.
- Snapshot auto-pruning (`--keep-last M`). Manual disk management
  acceptable in v1.
- Guest-driven cancel (spec open question 5).
- KVM/HVF-accelerated QEMU (spec section 5 row 9).
- Cross-ISA switching (spec section 5 row 11).

## References

- Design archive: `specs/hybrid-sim-gem5-switch-parity.md`
- Source-of-truth for trade-offs: `specs/gem5-cpu-model-switch.md`
- Transport choice rationale: `docs/simpoint_transport_comparison.md`
- Shared-memory alignment: `hybrid_plan.md` Section 9 (lines 462-590)
- Wire format: `include/hybrid/state_abi.h`

# Out-of-tree build for the QEMU TCG plugin libhybrid_handoff.so. Standalone
# from the vsim CMake build because the plugin links against host QEMU's
# headers + glib, neither of which exist inside the vsim build container.
#
# Usage:
#   make QEMU_ROOT=/local/nick/qemu_v5
#
# Output: libhybrid_handoff.so in this directory.

QEMU_ROOT ?= /local/nick/qemu_v5
CC        ?= gcc

GLIB_CFLAGS := $(shell pkg-config --cflags glib-2.0)
GLIB_LIBS   := $(shell pkg-config --libs   glib-2.0)

CFLAGS  := -O2 -fPIC -Wall -Wextra -std=c11
HYBRID_INCLUDE ?= ../include
INCLUDES := -I$(HYBRID_INCLUDE) -I$(QEMU_ROOT)/include/qemu $(GLIB_CFLAGS)
LDFLAGS := -shared
LIBS    := $(GLIB_LIBS)

.PHONY: all clean

all: libhybrid_handoff.so

libhybrid_handoff.so: hybrid_handoff.c \
                     $(HYBRID_INCLUDE)/hybrid/state_abi.h \
                     $(HYBRID_INCLUDE)/hybrid/state_abi_check.h \
                     $(HYBRID_INCLUDE)/hybrid/insn_match.h
	@if [ ! -f $(QEMU_ROOT)/include/qemu/qemu-plugin.h ]; then \
	    echo "error: qemu-plugin.h not under $(QEMU_ROOT)/include/qemu/"; \
	    echo "       set QEMU_ROOT=<path> on the make command line"; \
	    exit 1; \
	fi
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) $(INCLUDES) $(LDFLAGS) -o $@ $< $(LIBS)

clean:
	rm -f libhybrid_handoff.so

1	# SimPoint-driven hybrid sim: shared-mmap transport vs dump/reload transport
2	
3	Context: extend the existing QEMU + vsim/Verilator hybrid with a SimPoint-driven
4	workflow. End-to-end pipeline:
5	
6	1. **BBV generation** - run the workload in QEMU; emit Basic Block Vectors
7	   (one per fixed-size slice of dynamic instructions) into a `.bb` file.
8	2. **SimPoint clustering** - feed the `.bb` file into the SimPoint tool; it
9	   selects K representative slices and weights.
10	3. **Checkpoint collection** - replay the workload in QEMU, drain a checkpoint
11	   (architectural state + memory image) at each of the K simpoint boundaries.
12	4. **Detailed replay** - for each checkpoint, restore into vsim/Verilator and
13	   collect cycle-accurate metrics. Combine per-region metrics by weight.
14	
15	Steps 3 and 4 are exactly the QEMU -> vsim handoff we already have, just
16	repeated K times at SimPoint-selected boundaries instead of at WFI / CSR write.
17	The transport choice for that handoff matters: per-region snapshot cost
18	multiplies by K (often 10-30), and the trigger surface must accept "fire at
19	instruction count N", not just "fire at this PC".
20	
21	Two candidate transports exist in our tree today:
22	
23	1. The **shared-mmap** transport used by `hybrid_sim/` (this repo).
24	2. The **dump/reload** transport used by `/local/nick/vsim-demo-new/demo/hybrid`.
25	
26	This document compares them and recommends a path.
27	
28	---
29	
30	## 1. Mechanics side-by-side
31	
32	| Aspect | `hybrid_sim` (shared mmap) | `vsim-demo-new/demo/hybrid` (dump/reload) |
33	|---|---|---|
34	| Memory transport | Shared `/dev/shm` file mmap'd by QEMU1, vsim, QEMU2 via `-object memory-backend-file,share=on,...`. Zero-copy. | `dump-guest-memory` -> ELF core -> `tools/qemu_elfdump_to_main_memory.sh` extracts PT_LOAD 0 -> gzip -> vsim `--restore-memory <gz>`. |
35	| Arch state format | Binary `hybrid_state_v1` struct in `include/hybrid/state_abi.h`. Magic + version + ABI-locked offsets; layout enforced by `state_abi_check.h` static_asserts. | Text file `ae350regs.txt` with `REG`/`CSR`/`MMIO` lines; converted by `tools/regs2cmds.sh` into a GDB script of `set $csrN = 0x...` commands. |
36	| Drain mechanism | QEMU TCG plugin `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c` triggered on `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` or on ELF-symbol PC. Programmatic, no human in the loop. | Manual rendezvous on WFI: `Ctrl-A c` for `dump-guest-memory`, then attach GDB and `source tools/dump_ae350.gdb`. |
37	| Restore mechanism | vsim `ResumeDriver` (DMI Abstract Commands + Program Buffer); for QEMU2, `qemu_handback` spawns QEMU2 and pushes registers via gdbstub P-packets. | `./sim --restore-memory snap --reset-vector PC`, then attach GDB and `source ae350regs.cmds`, `set $dpc`, `continue`. |
38	| V extension support | Yes. V regs go through scratch GPA `0x80FF0000` via `vse8.v`; vtype/vl restored via Program Buffer `vsetvl`; vstart/vxsat/vxrm/vcsr via Abstract Cmds. | Not supported. `dump_ae350.gdb` lists only Andes V5 CSRs - no V regs and no V CSRs. |
39	| Trigger surface | `csrwi 0x7C0, 0/1` (default) or ELF-symbol PC (`_hybrid_enter_pc`/`_hybrid_exit_pc`). Both extensible from the TCG plugin (insn-count, region marker, ...). | WFI only; manual UI driving. Extending requires GDB scripting or QMP scripting. |
40	| Per-handoff latency | Microseconds: mmap is shared (no copy); state file is a few KB binary. | Seconds: 2 GiB gzip pass + ELF parsing + GDB scripting per drain and per restore. |
41	| Coupling between sides | Tight - both sides include the same C ABI header. | Loose - the GDB remote protocol is the only contract. |
42	| Auditability of snapshot | Binary; needs a viewer (or a converter to text). | Human-readable `ae350regs.txt` is the wire format. Easy to diff. |
43	| Persistence model | One live mmap file at a time; ephemeral. Snapshotting per region is `cp` (reflink-friendly). | One snapshot *directory* per checkpoint; archivable, portable, by construction. |
44	
45	Key file references in `hybrid_sim`:
46	
47	- `include/hybrid/state_abi.h` (wire struct)
48	- `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c` (drain on QEMU side; natural home for BBV emission too)
49	- `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/resume_driver.hpp` (restore into vsim via DMI)
50	- `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/state_drain.hpp` (drain on vsim side)
51	- `hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/rt_shared_mem.sh` (tier-2 harness using `memory-backend-file`)
52	
53	Key file references in the demo:
54	
55	- `demo/hybrid/main.c` (WFI-trigger fixture)
56	- `demo/hybrid/tools/dump_ae350.gdb` (GDB script that prints `ae350regs.txt`)
57	- `demo/hybrid/tools/regs2cmds.sh` (text -> GDB restore commands)
58	- `demo/hybrid/tools/qemu_elfdump_to_main_memory.sh` (ELF core -> gz raw)
59	- `demo/hybrid/README.md` (step-by-step instructions, ~140 lines)
60	
61	---
62	
63	## 2. Fit to a SimPoint pipeline driven from QEMU
64	
65	SimPoint needs three things from the host simulator:
66	
67	- **BBV emission**: per fixed-size slice (e.g. every 100 M insns), count how
68	  many times each static basic block was executed; dump as one row of the
69	  `.bb` file.
70	- **Insn-count trigger**: fire a drain when the dynamic instruction counter
71	  reaches a SimPoint-chosen value.
72	- **K-region replay**: K snapshots persisted to disk, each independently
73	  restorable.
74	
75	### 2.1 Shared mmap
76	
77	Strengths:
78	
79	- **BBV emission lives in the same plugin as the drain hook.** The QEMU TCG
80	  plugin already sees every translated TB and every executed insn; counting
81	  per-BB hits per slice and writing a row to a `.bb` file is a natural
82	  extension of `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c`. No new process, no GDB.
83	- **Insn-count trigger is a one-line addition** to the existing CSR/PC
84	  trigger logic in the plugin. The plugin already runs at retire time.
85	- **Per-snapshot drain is microseconds.** Taking K=10 (or K=30) checkpoints
86	  is effectively free relative to the simulation work.
87	- **Memory snapshot per region is a file copy of the mmap backing**:
88	  `cp --reflink=auto /dev/shm/mem snap/N/mem`. On btrfs/xfs this is
89	  O(metadata). On other filesystems it is one sequential read+write of RAM
90	  size, no compression on the critical path.
91	- **vsim restore path is unchanged.** `ResumeDriver` does not care whether
92	  `hybrid_state_v1` was emitted at a WFI, a CSR write, or an insn-count
93	  boundary.
94	- **V-state already validated end to end** through `rt_c_v_regs`,
95	  `rt_c_v_csrs` fixtures. SimPoint-driven runs of V workloads inherit this.
96	
97	Weaknesses:
98	
99	- A shared mmap is *live*. To collect K checkpoints we need to copy the
100	  mmap to a per-region file as part of the drain step; that is one extra
101	  syscall in the plugin but it must happen *before* QEMU resumes mutating
102	  the mmap. Solvable in the plugin (it is already the synchronization
103	  point) but a new code path.
104	- The binary ABI couples both sides: when state grows (new CSR, wider VLEN)
105	  the header bumps and producer + consumer rebuild in lock-step.
106	- vsim today reads memory live from the mmap. For checkpoint *replay* we
107	  need vsim to mmap the per-region snapshot file instead of the live one.
108	  This is a flag change in the tier-2 harness (`--shared-mem-path
109	  snap/N/mem`), not a structural change.
110	
111	### 2.2 Dump/reload
112	
113	Strengths:
114	
115	- Snapshots are *directories on disk* by construction. K checkpoints, K
116	  directories, no extra copy step.
117	- Loose coupling: schema drift between QEMU and vsim is absorbed in the awk
118	  filter (`void` and read-only entries are dropped).
119	- Human-readable text dump is a great debugging aid for a single mismatched
120	  CSR.
121	
122	Weaknesses:
123	
124	- **No BBV story.** GDB cannot see per-TB execution counts; `dump-guest-memory`
125	  is a memory dump, not an instrumentation hook. BBV emission would have to
126	  go in a TCG plugin anyway, at which point we are back to the mmap design
127	  for the BBV side and only using dump/reload for the snapshot side.
128	- **No insn-count trigger.** WFI is the only rendezvous today; reaching a
129	  SimPoint boundary by WFI would require injecting WFI traps into the
130	  workload, which is invasive and slow.
131	- **Per-region wall time is dominated by gzip of memory and GDB
132	  serialization.** For K=10 phases over a 2 GiB image, this is roughly two
133	  minutes of pure I/O per replay before any simulation begins.
134	- **V regs and V CSRs not supported.** Extending the text dump to cover them
135	  re-invents the V-state transport that the mmap path already has working.
136	- **GDB-as-orchestrator** means three or four shell processes per snapshot
137	  (QEMU, ICEman or vsim's gdbstub, GDB, vsim itself). Hard to make reliable
138	  across K regions in CI.
139	
140	### 2.3 Cost summary on a 2 GiB workload, K=10 regions
141	
142	| Stage | Shared mmap | Dump/reload |
143	|---|---|---|
144	| BBV emission (whole run) | in-plugin counter, one fwrite per slice (microseconds) | not natively supported; would need a parallel plugin anyway |
145	| Drain (per region) | ~0.05 ms (`state.bin` write) + cp/reflink of mmap | ~30 s (gzip 2 GiB) + ~2 s (GDB script) |
146	| Snapshot to disk (per region) | ~3 s `cp`, ~0 with reflink | already on disk |
147	| Restore into vsim (per region) | ~10 ms (DMI replay) + mmap open of snap | ~20 s (gunzip) + ~5 s (GDB replay) |
148	| Total per region | ~3 s worst case, ~10 ms with reflink | ~55 s |
149	| Total for K=10 | ~30 s worst case | ~10 min |
150	
151	Numbers are order-of-magnitude estimates, not measured. The point is the gap
152	is one to three decades, not 2x.
153	
154	---
155	
156	## 3. Recommendation
157	
158	Adopt the shared-mmap transport. Extend the existing TCG plugin and tier-2
159	harness; do not introduce the dump/reload path.
160	
161	### 3.1 What changes in `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c`
162	
163	1. **Add a BBV-emit mode** (`bbv_out=<path>,slice=<N>` plugin args). On each
164	   executed TB, increment a `bbv_t -> count` map keyed by translation-time
165	   BB id; every `slice` retired insns, write one row to the `.bb` file and
166	   reset the map. This is the standard SimPoint input format.
167	2. **Add an insn-count trigger** (`drain_at=<csv-of-N>` plugin arg). When the
168	   retired-insn counter reaches each value, run the same drain code path
169	   that the existing CSR/PC triggers use, and emit `state.bin` + the memory
170	   snapshot under `snap/region_<N>/`. The drain code already exists; only
171	   the trigger is new.
172	3. **Memory snapshot at drain time**: in the plugin, immediately before
173	   returning to QEMU, `cp --reflink=auto` (or plain `read/write`) the live
174	   mmap to `snap/region_<N>/mem`. This is the only new I/O code path.
175	
176	### 3.2 What changes in vsim
177	
178	Nothing structural. The tier-2 harness already accepts `--shared-mem-path`,
179	`--shared-mem-size`, `--shared-mem-base`. For replay, point it at
180	`snap/region_N/mem` and `--resume-from-qemu snap/region_N/state.bin`.
181	`ResumeDriver` is unchanged.
182	
183	### 3.3 What to keep from the demo
184	
185	The one feature worth borrowing is the **text pretty-printer** for snapshots.
186	Port `regs2cmds.sh` into a small tool that takes `hybrid_state_v1` (binary)
187	and prints `REG`/`CSR`/`MMIO` lines. Useful for diffing region N vs region N+1
188	when something looks wrong. Not on the critical path; ships alongside
189	snapshots as optional `state.txt`.
190	
191	### 3.4 What to drop from the demo entirely
192	
193	- gzip + ELF extraction of memory
194	- WFI as the only trigger
195	- text-as-wire-format
196	- GDB as the restore driver
197	
198	They were appropriate for a manual one-shot demo; they are wrong for
199	K-region replay.
200	
201	### 3.5 Final snapshot tree
202	
203	```
204	snap/
205	  bbv.bb                     # SimPoint input
206	  simpoints.txt              # SimPoint output (chosen region indices + weights)
207	  region_00/
208	    mem                      # mmap snapshot, restorable via memory-backend-file
209	    state.bin                # hybrid_state_v1
210	    state.txt                # optional pretty-print
211	  region_01/
212	    mem
213	    state.bin
214	    state.txt
215	  ...
216	```
217	
218	Each `region_N/` is independently consumable by the existing tier-2 harness.
219	
220	---
221	
222	## 4. Open questions before we commit
223	
224	- **Slice size for BBV emission**: SimPoint papers commonly use 100 M
225	  insns; we should sanity-check that against our workload lengths and
226	  cluster stability.
227	- **Insn counting precision in the plugin**: QEMU's
228	  `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_insn_exec_cb` fires per executed insn but
229	  may batch by TB; the exact retire-time counter that the drain reads must
230	  match the one the trigger compares against, or we drift across regions.
231	- **Multi-hart workloads**: BBVs and checkpoints are per-hart. Decide
232	  whether to extend `hybrid_state_v1` to multiple harts in the same file
233	  or to one file per hart per region.
234	- **Snapshot warmup**: cycle-accurate replay needs cache/TLB/branch-predictor
235	  warmup before the measurement window opens. Neither transport addresses
236	  this today; it belongs in a separate doc.
237	- **Region length for detailed sim**: Verilator throughput sets a practical
238	  upper bound on N (insns per measured region). We should benchmark vsim
239	  on a representative workload before fixing N in the SimPoint clustering.
240

file.content:
# SimPoint-driven hybrid sim: shared-mmap transport vs dump/reload transport

Context: extend the existing QEMU + vsim/Verilator hybrid with a SimPoint-driven
workflow. End-to-end pipeline:

1. **BBV generation** - run the workload in QEMU; emit Basic Block Vectors
   (one per fixed-size slice of dynamic instructions) into a `.bb` file.
2. **SimPoint clustering** - feed the `.bb` file into the SimPoint tool; it
   selects K representative slices and weights.
3. **Checkpoint collection** - replay the workload in QEMU, drain a checkpoint
   (architectural state + memory image) at each of the K simpoint boundaries.
4. **Detailed replay** - for each checkpoint, restore into vsim/Verilator and
   collect cycle-accurate metrics. Combine per-region metrics by weight.

Steps 3 and 4 are exactly the QEMU -> vsim handoff we already have, just
repeated K times at SimPoint-selected boundaries instead of at WFI / CSR write.
The transport choice for that handoff matters: per-region snapshot cost
multiplies by K (often 10-30), and the trigger surface must accept "fire at
instruction count N", not just "fire at this PC".

Two candidate transports exist in our tree today:

1. The **shared-mmap** transport used by `hybrid_sim/` (this repo).
2. The **dump/reload** transport used by `/local/nick/vsim-demo-new/demo/hybrid`.

This document compares them and recommends a path.

---

## 1. Mechanics side-by-side

| Aspect | `hybrid_sim` (shared mmap) | `vsim-demo-new/demo/hybrid` (dump/reload) |
|---|---|---|
| Memory transport | Shared `/dev/shm` file mmap'd by QEMU1, vsim, QEMU2 via `-object memory-backend-file,share=on,...`. Zero-copy. | `dump-guest-memory` -> ELF core -> `tools/qemu_elfdump_to_main_memory.sh` extracts PT_LOAD 0 -> gzip -> vsim `--restore-memory <gz>`. |
| Arch state format | Binary `hybrid_state_v1` struct in `include/hybrid/state_abi.h`. Magic + version + ABI-locked offsets; layout enforced by `state_abi_check.h` static_asserts. | Text file `ae350regs.txt` with `REG`/`CSR`/`MMIO` lines; converted by `tools/regs2cmds.sh` into a GDB script of `set $csrN = 0x...` commands. |
| Drain mechanism | QEMU TCG plugin `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c` triggered on `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` or on ELF-symbol PC. Programmatic, no human in the loop. | Manual rendezvous on WFI: `Ctrl-A c` for `dump-guest-memory`, then attach GDB and `source tools/dump_ae350.gdb`. |
| Restore mechanism | vsim `ResumeDriver` (DMI Abstract Commands + Program Buffer); for QEMU2, `qemu_handback` spawns QEMU2 and pushes registers via gdbstub P-packets. | `./sim --restore-memory snap --reset-vector PC`, then attach GDB and `source ae350regs.cmds`, `set $dpc`, `continue`. |
| V extension support | Yes. V regs go through scratch GPA `0x80FF0000` via `vse8.v`; vtype/vl restored via Program Buffer `vsetvl`; vstart/vxsat/vxrm/vcsr via Abstract Cmds. | Not supported. `dump_ae350.gdb` lists only Andes V5 CSRs - no V regs and no V CSRs. |
| Trigger surface | `csrwi 0x7C0, 0/1` (default) or ELF-symbol PC (`_hybrid_enter_pc`/`_hybrid_exit_pc`). Both extensible from the TCG plugin (insn-count, region marker, ...). | WFI only; manual UI driving. Extending requires GDB scripting or QMP scripting. |
| Per-handoff latency | Microseconds: mmap is shared (no copy); state file is a few KB binary. | Seconds: 2 GiB gzip pass + ELF parsing + GDB scripting per drain and per restore. |
| Coupling between sides | Tight - both sides include the same C ABI header. | Loose - the GDB remote protocol is the only contract. |
| Auditability of snapshot | Binary; needs a viewer (or a converter to text). | Human-readable `ae350regs.txt` is the wire format. Easy to diff. |
| Persistence model | One live mmap file at a time; ephemeral. Snapshotting per region is `cp` (reflink-friendly). | One snapshot *directory* per checkpoint; archivable, portable, by construction. |

Key file references in `hybrid_sim`:

- `include/hybrid/state_abi.h` (wire struct)
- `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c` (drain on QEMU side; natural home for BBV emission too)
- `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/resume_driver.hpp` (restore into vsim via DMI)
- `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/state_drain.hpp` (drain on vsim side)
- `hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/rt_shared_mem.sh` (tier-2 harness using `memory-backend-file`)

Key file references in the demo:

- `demo/hybrid/main.c` (WFI-trigger fixture)
- `demo/hybrid/tools/dump_ae350.gdb` (GDB script that prints `ae350regs.txt`)
- `demo/hybrid/tools/regs2cmds.sh` (text -> GDB restore commands)
- `demo/hybrid/tools/qemu_elfdump_to_main_memory.sh` (ELF core -> gz raw)
- `demo/hybrid/README.md` (step-by-step instructions, ~140 lines)

---

## 2. Fit to a SimPoint pipeline driven from QEMU

SimPoint needs three things from the host simulator:

- **BBV emission**: per fixed-size slice (e.g. every 100 M insns), count how
  many times each static basic block was executed; dump as one row of the
  `.bb` file.
- **Insn-count trigger**: fire a drain when the dynamic instruction counter
  reaches a SimPoint-chosen value.
- **K-region replay**: K snapshots persisted to disk, each independently
  restorable.

### 2.1 Shared mmap

Strengths:

- **BBV emission lives in the same plugin as the drain hook.** The QEMU TCG
  plugin already sees every translated TB and every executed insn; counting
  per-BB hits per slice and writing a row to a `.bb` file is a natural
  extension of `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c`. No new process, no GDB.
- **Insn-count trigger is a one-line addition** to the existing CSR/PC
  trigger logic in the plugin. The plugin already runs at retire time.
- **Per-snapshot drain is microseconds.** Taking K=10 (or K=30) checkpoints
  is effectively free relative to the simulation work.
- **Memory snapshot per region is a file copy of the mmap backing**:
  `cp --reflink=auto /dev/shm/mem snap/N/mem`. On btrfs/xfs this is
  O(metadata). On other filesystems it is one sequential read+write of RAM
  size, no compression on the critical path.
- **vsim restore path is unchanged.** `ResumeDriver` does not care whether
  `hybrid_state_v1` was emitted at a WFI, a CSR write, or an insn-count
  boundary.
- **V-state already validated end to end** through `rt_c_v_regs`,
  `rt_c_v_csrs` fixtures. SimPoint-driven runs of V workloads inherit this.

Weaknesses:

- A shared mmap is *live*. To collect K checkpoints we need to copy the
  mmap to a per-region file as part of the drain step; that is one extra
  syscall in the plugin but it must happen *before* QEMU resumes mutating
  the mmap. Solvable in the plugin (it is already the synchronization
  point) but a new code path.
- The binary ABI couples both sides: when state grows (new CSR, wider VLEN)
  the header bumps and producer + consumer rebuild in lock-step.
- vsim today reads memory live from the mmap. For checkpoint *replay* we
  need vsim to mmap the per-region snapshot file instead of the live one.
  This is a flag change in the tier-2 harness (`--shared-mem-path
  snap/N/mem`), not a structural change.

### 2.2 Dump/reload

Strengths:

- Snapshots are *directories on disk* by construction. K checkpoints, K
  directories, no extra copy step.
- Loose coupling: schema drift between QEMU and vsim is absorbed in the awk
  filter (`void` and read-only entries are dropped).
- Human-readable text dump is a great debugging aid for a single mismatched
  CSR.

Weaknesses:

- **No BBV story.** GDB cannot see per-TB execution counts; `dump-guest-memory`
  is a memory dump, not an instrumentation hook. BBV emission would have to
  go in a TCG plugin anyway, at which point we are back to the mmap design
  for the BBV side and only using dump/reload for the snapshot side.
- **No insn-count trigger.** WFI is the only rendezvous today; reaching a
  SimPoint boundary by WFI would require injecting WFI traps into the
  workload, which is invasive and slow.
- **Per-region wall time is dominated by gzip of memory and GDB
  serialization.** For K=10 phases over a 2 GiB image, this is roughly two
  minutes of pure I/O per replay before any simulation begins.
- **V regs and V CSRs not supported.** Extending the text dump to cover them
  re-invents the V-state transport that the mmap path already has working.
- **GDB-as-orchestrator** means three or four shell processes per snapshot
  (QEMU, ICEman or vsim's gdbstub, GDB, vsim itself). Hard to make reliable
  across K regions in CI.

### 2.3 Cost summary on a 2 GiB workload, K=10 regions

| Stage | Shared mmap | Dump/reload |
|---|---|---|
| BBV emission (whole run) | in-plugin counter, one fwrite per slice (microseconds) | not natively supported; would need a parallel plugin anyway |
| Drain (per region) | ~0.05 ms (`state.bin` write) + cp/reflink of mmap | ~30 s (gzip 2 GiB) + ~2 s (GDB script) |
| Snapshot to disk (per region) | ~3 s `cp`, ~0 with reflink | already on disk |
| Restore into vsim (per region) | ~10 ms (DMI replay) + mmap open of snap | ~20 s (gunzip) + ~5 s (GDB replay) |
| Total per region | ~3 s worst case, ~10 ms with reflink | ~55 s |
| Total for K=10 | ~30 s worst case | ~10 min |

Numbers are order-of-magnitude estimates, not measured. The point is the gap
is one to three decades, not 2x.

---

## 3. Recommendation

Adopt the shared-mmap transport. Extend the existing TCG plugin and tier-2
harness; do not introduce the dump/reload path.

### 3.1 What changes in `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c`

1. **Add a BBV-emit mode** (`bbv_out=<path>,slice=<N>` plugin args). On each
   executed TB, increment a `bbv_t -> count` map keyed by translation-time
   BB id; every `slice` retired insns, write one row to the `.bb` file and
   reset the map. This is the standard SimPoint input format.
2. **Add an insn-count trigger** (`drain_at=<csv-of-N>` plugin arg). When the
   retired-insn counter reaches each value, run the same drain code path
   that the existing CSR/PC triggers use, and emit `state.bin` + the memory
   snapshot under `snap/region_<N>/`. The drain code already exists; only
   the trigger is new.
3. **Memory snapshot at drain time**: in the plugin, immediately before
   returning to QEMU, `cp --reflink=auto` (or plain `read/write`) the live
   mmap to `snap/region_<N>/mem`. This is the only new I/O code path.

### 3.2 What changes in vsim

Nothing structural. The tier-2 harness already accepts `--shared-mem-path`,
`--shared-mem-size`, `--shared-mem-base`. For replay, point it at
`snap/region_N/mem` and `--resume-from-qemu snap/region_N/state.bin`.
`ResumeDriver` is unchanged.

### 3.3 What to keep from the demo

The one feature worth borrowing is the **text pretty-printer** for snapshots.
Port `regs2cmds.sh` into a small tool that takes `hybrid_state_v1` (binary)
and prints `REG`/`CSR`/`MMIO` lines. Useful for diffing region N vs region N+1
when something looks wrong. Not on the critical path; ships alongside
snapshots as optional `state.txt`.

### 3.4 What to drop from the demo entirely

- gzip + ELF extraction of memory
- WFI as the only trigger
- text-as-wire-format
- GDB as the restore driver

They were appropriate for a manual one-shot demo; they are wrong for
K-region replay.

### 3.5 Final snapshot tree

```
snap/
  bbv.bb                     # SimPoint input
  simpoints.txt              # SimPoint output (chosen region indices + weights)
  region_00/
    mem                      # mmap snapshot, restorable via memory-backend-file
    state.bin                # hybrid_state_v1
    state.txt                # optional pretty-print
  region_01/
    mem
    state.bin
    state.txt
  ...
```

Each `region_N/` is independently consumable by the existing tier-2 harness.

---

## 4. Open questions before we commit

- **Slice size for BBV emission**: SimPoint papers commonly use 100 M
  insns; we should sanity-check that against our workload lengths and
  cluster stability.
- **Insn counting precision in the plugin**: QEMU's
  `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_insn_exec_cb` fires per executed insn but
  may batch by TB; the exact retire-time counter that the drain reads must
  match the one the trigger compares against, or we drift across regions.
- **Multi-hart workloads**: BBVs and checkpoints are per-hart. Decide
  whether to extend `hybrid_state_v1` to multiple harts in the same file
  or to one file per hart per region.
- **Snapshot warmup**: cycle-accurate replay needs cache/TLB/branch-predictor
  warmup before the measurement window opens. Neither transport addresses
  this today; it belongs in a separate doc.
- **Region length for detailed sim**: Verilator throughput sets a practical
  upper bound on N (insns per measured region). We should benchmark vsim
  on a representative workload before fixing N in the SimPoint clustering.

Based on the search results, I found information about SimPoint 3.2 and its source code:

SimPoint 3.2 includes a README file, input directory with sample files, output directory for storing results, analysiscode directory containing C++ code, and a bin directory containing the simpoint executable. The Makefile provides commands to build the SimPoint program for clustering analysis.

SimPoint performs analysis on frequency vector execution history of programs, using basic block vectors as an example format. The analysis involves running a program and recording frequency vectors for each execution interval, then analyzing these vectors to return ideal simulation points and the duration of the initialization phase.

A GitHub repository for SimPoint 3.2 is available at: https://github.com/OscarLGH/SimPoint.3.2

Additionally, the official UCSD SimPoint website (http://www-cse.ucsd.edu/~calder/simpoint/) provides packages to generate basic block profiles, which are used as input for the SimPoint analysis tool.

content.content:
# SimPoint 3.2 Build Process and Modifications

## Build Process

The repository includes a Makefile with three commands:

- `make simpoint` - builds the clustering analysis program
- `make all` - generates the program and runs it on sample input
- `make clean` - removes build artifacts

"The simpoint binary is copied into the bin directory" after compilation.

## Patches and Fixes Applied

According to the release notes (February 1, 2006), this version addressed several issues:

- **64-bit compilation bug fix** - resolved problems on AMD64 and PowerPC/OSX architectures
- **Performance optimization** - unrolled the inner k-means loop
- **Random number generator** - implemented a custom RNG for consistent results across platforms
- **Code cleanup** - removed unused code from Datapoint/Dataset classes
- **K-means bug fix** - corrected incorrect output when zero iterations were selected

## Modern GCC Considerations

The documentation does not explicitly address modern GCC compatibility issues. However, the 2006 release notes indicate attention to 64-bit compiler concerns. The codebase is written in C++ (69.4% of repository) and appears to be over 15 years old, which typically suggests potential compatibility concerns with current compiler versions, though none are documented here.

content.text:
The sandbox blocked cloning external SimPoint source for build/run, citing untrusted code integration. I need your authorization to proceed.

**What I want to do:**
1. Clone https://github.com/OscarLGH/SimPoint.3.2.git into `tools/simpoint/` (mirror of original UCSD SimPoint 3.2 source from 2006; ~5 KLOC of C++ clustering code).
2. Build it with `make` (produces a single `bin/simpoint` binary).
3. Run it on a hand-crafted BBV first to verify it works, then on a real BBV emitted from our QEMU plugin.

**Alternative paths if you prefer not to clone:**
- Use the original UCSD tarball `simpoint.3.2.tar.gz` from `https://cseweb.ucsd.edu/~calder/simpoint/` (same code, just packaged).
- Skip the actual SimPoint binary build this session; instead, scope this session to F7 (BBV emission) only, and defer the SimPoint binary integration to a later session with a separate ROADMAP entry I'll add.

**Recommendation:** Option 1 (clone mirror) is cleanest. SimPoint is a published academic tool and the mirror is widely used in the simulation community.

Please choose: clone-and-build, tarball-fetch, or defer SimPoint-binary work.

Sources:
- [SimPoint 3.2 GitHub mirror](https://github.com/OscarLGH/SimPoint.3.2)
- [UCSD SimPoint home (original source)](https://cseweb.ucsd.edu/~calder/simpoint/)
- [SimPoint 3.0 release notes](https://cseweb.ucsd.edu/~calder/simpoint/simpoint-3-0.htm)
